county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Newton County formed 1836 from Neshoba County. County seat is Decatur. Located in east-central Mississippi near the Alabama border. Named for Sergeant John Newton of the Revolutionary War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Newton County (formed 1836 from Neshoba) has Decatur as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Newton County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. Newton County is in east-central Mississippi near the Alabama border. BLM GLO land patents document original land entries. The county had a mixed economy of cotton farming and timber. Some Choctaw families remained in Newton County after removal. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Newton County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the late 1830s document early families. The Newton County Historical and Genealogical Society maintains local indexes.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Newton County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Decatur maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Noxubee County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Macon. One of the wealthiest antebellum cotton counties in Mississippi. The name is Choctaw for 'stinking water.'
county_metadata.research_tips: Noxubee County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Macon as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. MDAH has extensive microfilm of Noxubee County records. Noxubee County was one of Mississippi's wealthiest antebellum cotton counties with an enslaved population that vastly outnumbered whites. Plantation records, slave schedules, and estate inventories at MDAH are essential for African American research. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Macon sub-district are extensive. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. Noxubee County has the oldest African American church congregations in the area. FamilySearch has digitized Noxubee County clerk records. The Noxubee County Library has a local history collection.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Noxubee County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Macon maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Oktibbeha County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Starkville, home to Mississippi State University (founded 1878). The name is Choctaw for 'bloody ice (water).'
county_metadata.research_tips: Oktibbeha County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Starkville as county seat, home to Mississippi State University. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Oktibbeha County records. The Mitchell Memorial Library at Mississippi State University holds the Congressional and Political Research Center with regional manuscript collections. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. The county had a significant enslaved population. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. Mississippi A&M (now MSU) was founded in 1878, and early student and employee records are at the university archives. FamilySearch has digitized Oktibbeha County clerk records. Church records from Starkville Baptist and Methodist congregations from the 1830s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Oktibbeha County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Starkville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Panola County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Batesville, with two judicial districts (Batesville and Sardis). Straddles the Delta and hill regions. The name is Choctaw for 'cotton.'
county_metadata.research_tips: Panola County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) has Batesville as county seat, with Sardis as the other major town (two judicial districts). The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Panola County land records, chancery minutes, and marriage books. Panola County straddles the Delta and hill regions. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. The county had a large enslaved population in the western (Delta) portion. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the county. FamilySearch has digitized Panola County clerk records. The Panola County Historical and Genealogical Society maintains indexes. Baptist and Methodist church records from the late 1830s are important sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Panola County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Batesville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pearl River County formed 1890 from Hancock and Marion counties. County seat is Poplarville. Located in south Mississippi in the Piney Woods. Named for the Pearl River. Pearl River Community College was founded in 1909.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pearl River County (formed 1890 from Hancock and Marion) has Poplarville as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1890. For pre-1890 records, check Hancock County (Bay St. Louis) and Marion County (Columbia -- note the 1881 fire). MDAH has microfilm of Pearl River County records. Pearl River County is in the Piney Woods with a timber and tung oil economy. Pearl River Community College (1909) records may be useful. FamilySearch has some Pearl River County records. Baptist church records are the primary denominational source. Tax rolls at MDAH document landholding families. The Pearl River and its tributaries influenced settlement patterns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Pearl River County deed books from 1890; Chancery Clerk in Poplarville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1897 courthouse fire destroyed most early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Perry County formed 1820 from Greene County. County seat is New Augusta. Burned county: 1897 courthouse fire destroyed most early records. Located in the Piney Woods. Named for Commodore Oliver Hazard Perry.
county_metadata.research_tips: Perry County (formed 1820 from Greene) is a burned county due to the 1897 courthouse fire that destroyed most early records. County seat is New Augusta. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed post-fire records. For the burned period (1820-1897), use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), Greene County records for pre-1820 entries, and MDAH microfilm. Perry County is in the Piney Woods with a timber economy. Camp Shelby (est. 1917) is partly in Perry County -- military records at NARA are relevant. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. Census records from 1830 forward are available. FamilySearch has limited Perry County records. Baptist church records supplement the burned civil records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Perry County deed books from 1820; Chancery Clerk in New Augusta maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pike County formed 1815 from Marion County. County seat is Magnolia, with McComb as the larger city. Located in southwest Mississippi. McComb was significant in the civil rights movement. Named for Zebulon Pike.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pike County (formed 1815 from Marion) has Magnolia as county seat, with McComb as the larger city. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1815 and chancery court records. Pike County has well-preserved early records. MDAH has microfilm of Pike County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. BLM GLO federal land patents document early entries. Pike County was significant in the civil rights movement -- McComb was a center of SNCC voter registration activism. Freedmen's Bureau records for southwest Mississippi cover Pike County. The Illinois Central Railroad made McComb a division point. FamilySearch has digitized Pike County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1820s document early families. The Pike County Historical Society maintains local collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Pike County deed books from 1815; Chancery Clerk in Magnolia maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pontotoc County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832 (Treaty of Pontotoc). County seat is Pontotoc. The town was the principal Chickasaw settlement and site of the Chickasaw Council House. The name is Chickasaw for 'cattails growing on the prairie.'
county_metadata.research_tips: Pontotoc County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) has Pontotoc as county seat. The county is named for the Treaty of Pontotoc (1832), which ceded Chickasaw lands. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Pontotoc County records. The Chickasaw Council House site is in Pontotoc -- the town was the principal Chickasaw settlement. Chickasaw removal records at NARA (Record Group 75) are essential for Native American research. BLM GLO land patents document the federal land sales that followed removal. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Pontotoc County clerk records. Church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations from the late 1830s document early settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Pontotoc County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Pontotoc maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Pontotoc County from 1840 through 1950; includes slave schedules 1850-1860 listing enslaved persons by owner; agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide economic data; the 1890 census was destroyed nationally

source_profiles.source_name: Pontotoc County Chickasaw Cession and Removal Records -- NARA / BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: NARA (Record Group 75) / Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pontotoc County was formed from Chickasaw Cession lands (Treaty of Pontotoc, 1832). BLM GLO records document federal land sales following removal. NARA Record Group 75 holds Chickasaw removal rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence

source_profiles.source_name: Pontotoc County Military Records -- MDAH / NARA / Fold3
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / NARA / Fold3
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil War Confederate service records for Pontotoc County soldiers; Mississippi contributed heavily to the Confederate army; MDAH holds state adjutant general records; NARA has compiled Confederate service records; Fold3 has digitized many; also includes WWI/WWII draft registrations

source_profiles.source_name: Pontotoc County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / MSGenWeb
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / MSGenWeb / Local historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Pontotoc County churches, community cemeteries, and family burial grounds; Find A Grave has growing coverage; MSGenWeb volunteers have transcribed many Pontotoc County cemeteries; particularly valuable when civil records are incomplete or destroyed

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Prentiss County formed 1870 from Tishomingo County. County seat is Booneville. Located in northeast Mississippi hill country. Named for Sergeant Smith Prentiss, renowned Mississippi orator.
county_metadata.research_tips: Prentiss County (formed 1870 from Tishomingo) has Booneville as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1870. For pre-1870 records, check Tishomingo County (Iuka -- note Civil War damage). MDAH has microfilm of Prentiss County records. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. Prentiss County is in northeast Mississippi with a small-farm economy. The Battle of Brice's Cross Roads (1864) occurred nearby in Lee County. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Prentiss County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records are important sources. Tax rolls at MDAH trace landholding families from 1870.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Prentiss County deed books from 1870; Chancery Clerk in Booneville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Prentiss County Deed Books -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Prentiss County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Prentiss County from 1870; MDAH holds microfilm copies; Chancery Clerk in False maintains originals; Mississippi is a public land state with BLM GLO patents available online
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1870; original deed books held by Chancery Clerk in False

source_profiles.source_name: Prentiss County Chancery Court Records (Probate) -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Prentiss County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mississippi uses Chancery Court for probate (unique system -- no separate probate court). Wills, estates, guardianships, and land partitions for Prentiss County from 1870; MDAH has microfilm of chancery minutes and estate files

source_profiles.source_name: Prentiss County Marriage Records -- Chancery Clerk / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Prentiss County Chancery Clerk, False / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Prentiss County from 1870; FamilySearch has indexed many Mississippi county marriage records; state marriage registration began 1926 through Mississippi State Department of Health

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Quitman County formed 1877 from Coahoma, Panola, Tallahatchie, and Tunica counties. County seat is Marks. A Delta county. Marks was the starting point of the 1968 Poor People's Campaign. Named for Governor John A. Quitman.
county_metadata.research_tips: Quitman County (formed 1877 from Coahoma, Panola, Tallahatchie, and Tunica) has Marks as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1877. For pre-1877 records, check the four parent counties. MDAH has microfilm of Quitman County records. Quitman County is a Delta county with a historically large African American population on cotton plantations. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Delta region are essential. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. The 1927 Great Flood impacted Quitman County -- Red Cross records at NARA are useful. Marks was the starting point of the 1968 Poor People's Campaign. FamilySearch has some Quitman County records. Levee board records document families along waterways.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Quitman County deed books from 1877; Chancery Clerk in Marks maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Quitman County Deed Books -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Quitman County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson

source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1877; original deed books held by Chancery Clerk in False

source_profiles.repository: Quitman County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson

source_profiles.source_name: Quitman County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Quitman County from 1880 through 1950; includes slave schedules 1850-1860 listing enslaved persons by owner; agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide economic data; the 1890 census was destroyed nationally

source_profiles.source_name: Quitman County Enslaved Persons and Plantation Records -- MDAH / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Quitman County had a very large enslaved population. MDAH holds plantation records, estate inventories listing enslaved persons by name, slave schedules (1850, 1860), and slaveholder correspondence. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA (M826, M1907) document formerly enslaved families post-war

source_profiles.source_name: Quitman County Tax Rolls -- MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax rolls for Quitman County listing property owners, acreage, livestock, and enslaved persons (pre-1865); essential for tracking family landholding and wealth; MDAH holds microfilm of county tax rolls
